graph.topic: Uncovering Clinical Intuition and Bottlenecks in Early Neurocognitive Disorder (NCD) Screening
graph.researcher_bg: An HCI researcher specializing in Healthcare and Accessibility, focused on designing high-ecological-validity digital tasks (e.g., virtual grocery shopping) to augment early-stage cognitive decline screening.
graph.expert_bg: A seasoned clinical neuropsychologist or geriatrician with extensive experience in conducting baseline cognitive assessments (like MoCA or MMSE) , currently navigating the challenges of unstructured daily task observation and unpacking clinical intuition for early neurocognitive diagnosis.

q_01__exp_L2_00_00.label: Administration Dynamics & Patient Engagement
q_01__exp_L2_00_00.description: The real-world challenges of physically administering the test and managing the patient's state during the workflow.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_00.label: Rapport-building as diagnostic baseline
q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_00.description: I start with a quick rapport-building chat about their day; if they can't recall recent changes in mood or memory during the chat, the test hasn't even started and I already have my first clue.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_01.label: Adjusting pace based on frustration cues
q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_01.description: If I see them starting to sweat or get quiet during the serial 7s, I know I need to pivot or soften my tone, otherwise they'll just shut down and I'll lose them for the rest of the test.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_02.label: Ensuring quiet environment for focus
q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_02.description: I try to go through the MoCA or MMSE in a quiet room, making sure they're not distracted, because if the phone rings or someone walks in, I can't tell if it's distraction or deficit.

q_01__exp_L2_00_01.label: Clinical Interpretation & Validity
q_01__exp_L2_00_01.description: The mental work of deciding whether the score actually reflects brain health or something else.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_00.label: Behavioral process and "in-between" moments
q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_00.description: It's not just about the score—it's about the process. I'll note things like how long they take to answer, if they hesitate, or even what they do when they're just sitting there thinking.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_01.label: Probing beyond the standardized script
q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_01.description: The MoCA and MMSE are tools, but they're not the whole picture. I don't just stick to the script; real clues come when I start probing beyond it or when they're not expecting it.

q_01__exp_L2_00_02.label: Contextual & Longitudinal Factors
q_01__exp_L2_00_02.description: Factors outside the immediate test score that influence diagnostic confidence.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_00.label: Score vs. Real-World Function
q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_00.description: They might score a 26 which looks 'normal', but if their daughter says they got lost driving home or they can't manage meds, I trust the real-life function over the score.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_01.label: Longitudinal Knowledge of Patient
q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_01.description: And honestly, a lot of it is just knowing the person over time; patterns and inconsistencies show up when you've seen them before, not just on paper.

q_01__res_L1_00.label: Operational Workflow & Task Sequence
q_01__res_L1_00.description: Examining the step-by-step procedural logic and interaction flow during the administration of standardized cognitive tests.

q_01__res_L2_00_00.label: Administration Protocol Logic
q_01__res_L2_00_00.description: Understanding the sequential rules and branching logic inherent in the test workflow.

q_01__res_leaf_00_00_00.label: Fixed vs. adaptive sequencing of sub-tests
q_01__res_leaf_00_00_00.description: Identify whether the order of assessment components (e.g., orientation before recall) is rigidly fixed or dynamically adjusted based on patient performance.

q_01__res_leaf_00_00_01.label: Criteria for transitioning between test sections
q_01__res_leaf_00_00_01.description: Determine the specific triggers or completion markers that signal the clinician to move from one cognitive domain task to the next.

q_01__res_L2_00_01.label: Instructional Interaction
q_01__res_L2_00_01.description: Analyzing how test instructions are delivered and managed between clinician and patient.

q_01__res_leaf_00_01_00.label: Standardization of verbal prompts
q_01__res_leaf_00_01_00.description: Explore the degree of script fidelity required versus the allowance for spontaneous clarification during formal scoring.

q_01__res_leaf_00_01_01.label: Protocol for instruction repetition
q_01__res_leaf_00_01_01.description: Uncover the rules governing how many times or in what manner instructions can be repeated without invalidating the test score.

q_01__res_L1_01.label: Artifact Interaction & Data Capture
q_01__res_L1_01.description: Investigating the physical and digital tools used during assessment and how data is recorded from them.

q_01__res_L2_01_00.label: Physical-Digital Tooling
q_01__res_L2_01_00.description: Mapping the tangible artifacts and sensory materials required to execute the assessment.

q_01__res_leaf_01_00_00.label: Artifacts for visuospatial tasks
q_01__res_leaf_01_00_00.description: Catalog the specific physical tools (e.g., pen types, paper grids, clock faces) used for drawing-based sub-tests and their affordances.

q_01__res_leaf_01_00_01.label: Visibility constraints of stimuli
q_01__res_leaf_01_00_01.description: Assess how test materials (cards, screens) are presented to accommodate varying levels of visual acuity during the task.

q_01__res_L2_01_01.label: Scoring & Documentation Workflow
q_01__res_L2_01_01.description: Understanding the timing and method of converting patient performance into recorded clinical data.

q_01__res_leaf_01_01_00.label: Timing of score recording
q_01__res_leaf_01_01_00.description: Determine if scoring occurs concurrently with patient interaction or retrospectively after the session concludes.

q_01__res_leaf_01_01_01.label: Data transfer method to EHR
q_01__res_leaf_01_01_01.description: Identify the mechanism for moving raw test scores into the Electronic Health Record (manual entry, scanning, digital sync).

q_01__res_L1_02.label: Environmental Configuration & Accessibility
q_01__res_L1_02.description: Exploring the physical setting and accommodations made to ensure valid test conditions.

q_01__res_L2_02_00.label: Spatial & Sensory Configuration
q_01__res_L2_02_00.description: Examining how the physical room and patient capabilities are managed to support the assessment workflow.

q_01__res_leaf_02_00_00.label: Seating arrangement relative to test materials
q_01__res_leaf_02_00_00.description: Document the geometric setup between clinician, patient, and test artifacts to ensure optimal visibility and interaction.

q_01__res_leaf_02_00_01.label: Protocol for integrating assistive devices
q_01__res_leaf_02_00_01.description: Clarify the workflow steps for verifying and utilizing patient aids (glasses, hearing aids) before starting the scored portion.

assistance_payload.validate_focus: The expert emphasizes that diagnostic insight begins during pre-test rapport building rather than the formal assessment. This perspective highlights how patient engagement prior to testing serves as a crucial baseline for clinical intuition.

pivot.limitation: Focusing solely on the rapport phase leaves the structural logic of the formal assessment protocol unexamined. Without understanding the test sequencing, it remains unclear how the tool itself accommodates clinical flexibility.
pivot.research_goal: The researcher is trying to understand whether the official administration protocol dictates a rigid order of sub-tests or allows dynamic adjustment based on patient performance.
pivot.compelling_reason: Both the pre-test observation and the formal test structure are necessary to understand the full workflow. Knowing the sequencing rules helps determine if the tool supports the clinical intuition established during rapport.
pivot.coarse_scenario: While I see how much diagnostic value you get from that initial chat, I also need to understand how the formal test structure itself supports or constrains those observations once you move past the rapport building.

q_01__exp_L2_00_00.label: Administration Dynamics & Patient Engagement
q_01__exp_L2_00_00.description: The real-world challenges of physically administering the test and managing the patient's state during the workflow.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_00.label: Rapport-building as diagnostic baseline
q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_00.description: I start with a quick rapport-building chat about their day; if they can't recall recent changes in mood or memory during the chat, the test hasn't even started and I already have my first clue.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_01.label: Adjusting pace based on frustration cues
q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_01.description: If I see them starting to sweat or get quiet during the serial 7s, I know I need to pivot or soften my tone, otherwise they'll just shut down and I'll lose them for the rest of the test.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_02.label: Ensuring quiet environment for focus
q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_02.description: I try to go through the MoCA or MMSE in a quiet room, making sure they're not distracted, because if the phone rings or someone walks in, I can't tell if it's distraction or deficit.

q_01__exp_L2_00_01.label: Clinical Interpretation & Validity
q_01__exp_L2_00_01.description: The mental work of deciding whether the score actually reflects brain health or something else.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_00.label: Behavioral process and "in-between" moments
q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_00.description: It's not just about the score—it's about the process. I'll note things like how long they take to answer, if they hesitate, or even what they do when they're just sitting there thinking.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_01.label: Probing beyond the standardized script
q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_01.description: The MoCA and MMSE are tools, but they're not the whole picture. I don't just stick to the script; real clues come when I start probing beyond it or when they're not expecting it.

q_01__exp_L2_00_02.label: Contextual & Longitudinal Factors
q_01__exp_L2_00_02.description: Factors outside the immediate test score that influence diagnostic confidence.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_00.label: Score vs. Real-World Function
q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_00.description: They might score a 26 which looks 'normal', but if their daughter says they got lost driving home or they can't manage meds, I trust the real-life function over the score.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_01.label: Longitudinal Knowledge of Patient
q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_01.description: And honestly, a lot of it is just knowing the person over time; patterns and inconsistencies show up when you've seen them before, not just on paper.

q_02__exp_L1_00.description: Interview topic: Uncovering Clinical Intuition and Bottlenecks in Early Neurocognitive Disorder (NCD) Screening

q_02__exp_L2_00_00.label: Diagnostic Ambiguity & Clinical Gray Zones
q_02__exp_L2_00_00.description: The inherent difficulty in separating normal aging processes from early pathological changes when test scores aren't definitive.

q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_00.label: The Benign Forgetfulness Trap
q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_00.description: It's tough to tell if forgetting keys is just normal aging or the start of something worse. Scores often look identical, but the patient's insight feels different.

q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_01.label: Test Scores vs. Clinical Reality
q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_01.description: Standard screens give a number but miss the nuance of real-world function or conversation. You know they're struggling, but the MoCA says 26/30. Day-to-day performance varies too much for a snapshot.

q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_02.label: Spouse Filtering of Symptoms
q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_02.description: Sometimes the husband hides the wife's mistakes to protect her dignity, or exaggerates them because he's burnt out. You never know which story you're getting.

q_02__exp_L2_00_01.label: The Human Element & Patient Dynamics
q_02__exp_L2_00_01.description: Emotional and behavioral factors that interfere with accurate assessment during the consultation.

q_02__exp_leaf_00_01_00.label: White Coat Anxiety Masking Ability
q_02__exp_leaf_00_01_00.description: They get so nervous testing makes them fail. You have to sense when it's panic vs. deficit, otherwise you over-diagnose.

q_02__exp_leaf_00_01_01.label: Active Resistance or Denial
q_02__exp_leaf_00_01_01.description: Some patients refuse to engage due to fear, or simply lack insight and minimize symptoms. They guess randomly or act like everything's fine to shut the test down.

q_02__exp_leaf_00_01_02.label: The 'Look' of Confusion vs. Apathy
q_02__exp_leaf_00_01_02.description: Sometimes you just see a blank stare or hesitation that isn't in the score sheet. It tells you something is wrong, but it's hard to document or justify.

q_02__exp_L2_00_02.label: Operational & Workflow Bottlenecks
q_02__exp_L2_00_02.description: Systemic constraints that limit the depth and quality of the screening process.

q_02__exp_leaf_00_02_00.label: Rushed Baseline Due to Schedule
q_02__exp_leaf_00_02_00.description: You only have 20 minutes to build rapport and test them while juggling a dozen other things. You can't really warm them up, so the baseline is cold and often inaccurate.

q_03__shared_root.label: Uncovering Clinical Intuition and Bottlenecks in Early Neurocognitive Disorder (NCD) Screening
q_03__shared_root.description: Interview topic: Uncovering Clinical Intuition and Bottlenecks in Early Neurocognitive Disorder (NCD) Screening

q_03__exp_L1_00.label: Ecological Observation Mindset
q_03__exp_L1_00.description: The mental framework and preparatory considerations required before and during the observation of real-world tasks.

q_03__exp_L2_00_00.label: Managing the Observation Environment
q_03__exp_L2_00_00.description: Balancing the need for natural behavior with the artificiality of being watched and safety constraints.

q_03__exp_leaf_00_00_00.label: The 'Audience Effect' on Performance
q_03__exp_leaf_00_00_00.description: They turn into an actor the moment I pull out the recipe card. If they keep checking my face while pouring the water, they're performing, not functioning. I have to sense when they forget I'm there to get real data.

q_03__exp_leaf_00_00_01.label: Safety vs. Diagnostic Integrity
q_03__exp_leaf_00_00_01.description: Do I let them grab the sharp knife if their hand is shaking just to see if they can manage it? I need the data on their motor planning, but I can't let them get hurt. It's a constant tug-of-war between safety and truth, and knowing exactly when to step in.

q_03__exp_L2_00_01.label: Establishing the Personal Baseline
q_03__exp_L2_00_01.description: Contextualizing current performance against the patient's historical capabilities and task familiarity.

q_03__exp_leaf_00_01_00.label: Premorbid Skill Context
q_03__exp_leaf_00_01_00.description: Was this person a chef or did they burn water before the dementia? If I don't know their 'before', I can't judge their 'now'. A messy kitchen might be normal for them, not a symptom, or they might just be unfamiliar with this specific task.

q_03__exp_L1_01.label: The Execution Bottleneck
q_03__exp_L1_01.description: Specific phases within the task performance where clinical judgment becomes most difficult or ambiguous.

q_03__exp_L2_01_00.label: Executive Load & Transitions
q_03__exp_L2_01_00.description: The critical moments between steps or during sustained effort where cognitive load spikes and deficits often reveal themselves.

q_03__exp_leaf_01_00_00.label: Execution Pauses & Flow Hesitation
q_03__exp_leaf_01_00_00.description: It's that split second between chopping the onion and finding the pan, OR the hesitation while chopping. If they just stand there holding the knife or move with uncertain slowness, the engine has stalled or is struggling. That pause or flow-hesitation is where the executive function dies.

q_03__exp_leaf_01_00_01.label: Error Monitoring & Insight
q_03__exp_leaf_01_00_01.description: They put salt in twice. Do they catch it? If they taste it and shrug, that's one thing. If they don't even notice the shaker is empty, that's insight loss. Watching them catch their own mistakes is gold, especially since they might not realize it felt hard.

q_03__exp_L2_01_01.label: Environmental Interference
q_03__exp_L2_01_01.description: Distinguishing between external distractions, internal fatigue, and cognitive deficits during the task.

q_03__exp_leaf_01_01_00.label: Distraction Attribution
q_03__exp_leaf_01_01_00.description: The phone rings. Do they answer it mid-pour? Or ignore it? Hard to tell if it's impulse control issues, fatigue, or just normal distraction. In a clinic, it's quiet; in a kitchen, life happens, and I have to know which is which.

q_03__exp_L1_02.label: Diagnostic Synthesis & Judgment
q_03__exp_L1_02.description: The final phase of interpreting observed behaviors into a clinical conclusion.

q_03__exp_L2_02_00.label: Masking & Compensation
q_03__exp_L2_02_00.description: Identifying when successful task completion hides underlying cognitive failure through alternative strategies.

q_03__exp_leaf_02_00_00.label: The 'Workaround' Mask
q_03__exp_leaf_02_00_00.description: They might fail the memory part but smell every spice to compensate. It looks like success, but it's actually a workaround hiding the deficit. I have to spot the inefficient strategy that's masking the brain fog.

q_03__exp_L2_02_01.label: Emotional & Behavioral Response
q_03__exp_L2_02_01.description: Evaluating the patient's emotional regulation as a marker of cognitive load and frontal lobe function.

q_03__exp_leaf_02_01_00.label: Frustration Tolerance
q_03__exp_leaf_02_01_00.description: Do they cry when the soup burns, or just laugh it off? The emotional reaction tells me more than the burnt soup. If they explode over a spilled drop, their frontal lobes are likely struggling to regulate the stress of the task.

q_03__res_L1_00.label: Behavioral Markers in Ecological Tasks
q_03__res_L1_00.description: Investigating the specific micro-behaviors clinicians prioritize when observing unstructured daily tasks versus standardized tests.

q_03__res_L2_00_00.label: Critical Failure Modes & Error Types
q_03__res_L2_00_00.description: Identifying which specific errors in a task (e.g., grocery shopping) carry the most diagnostic weight for clinicians.

q_03__res_leaf_00_00_00.label: Differentiate omission vs. commission errors
q_03__res_leaf_00_00_00.description: Determine if clinicians view forgetting an item (omission) as more indicative of decline than picking the wrong item (commission) during task execution.

q_03__res_leaf_00_00_01.label: Weighting of semantic vs. procedural failures
q_03__res_leaf_00_00_01.description: Explore whether breaking the logic of the task (e.g., buying soap instead of food) is judged more severely than mechanical struggles (e.g., UI navigation).

q_03__res_L2_00_01.label: Error Recovery & Strategy Observation
q_03__res_L2_00_01.description: Understanding how clinicians interpret a patient's ability to self-correct during unstructured tasks.

q_03__res_leaf_00_01_00.label: Value of self-correction behaviors
q_03__res_leaf_00_01_00.description: Assess if a patient recognizing and fixing their own mistake mitigates clinical concern compared to unawareness of the error.

q_03__res_leaf_00_01_01.label: Observation of compensatory strategies
q_03__res_leaf_00_01_01.description: Identify if clinicians note external aids (e.g., writing lists) used during the task as positive adaptation or negative dependency.

q_03__res_L1_01.label: Temporal Dynamics of Clinical Confidence
q_03__res_L1_01.description: Mapping how clinician confidence fluctuates throughout the phases of an ecological task to pinpoint bottlenecks in judgment.

q_03__res_L2_01_00.label: Phase-Specific Judgment Difficulty
q_03__res_L2_01_00.description: Pinpointing exactly which stage of the task (initiation, execution, completion) creates the most ambiguity for the observer.

q_03__res_leaf_01_00_00.label: Identify the confidence tipping point
q_03__res_leaf_01_00_00.description: Determine the specific moment in the task flow where a clinician shifts from 'monitoring' to 'suspecting pathology'.

q_03__res_leaf_01_00_01.label: Ambiguity in task initiation phases
q_03__res_leaf_01_00_01.description: Investigate if the setup phase (understanding instructions) causes more diagnostic uncertainty than the active performance phase.

q_03__res_L2_01_01.label: Longitudinal Observation Needs
q_03__res_L2_01_01.description: Understanding if a single task instance is sufficient or if clinicians require trend data to feel confident.

q_03__res_leaf_01_01_00.label: Single-session vs. multi-session validity
q_03__res_leaf_01_01_00.description: Explore whether clinicians distrust ecological task results unless they are repeated over time to rule out bad days.

q_03__res_L1_02.label: Interface-Mediated Assessment Validity
q_03__res_L1_02.description: Examining how the digital medium itself influences the clinician's ability to distinguish between tech-literacy and cognitive decline.

q_03__res_L2_02_00.label: Digital Literacy Confounds
q_03__res_L2_02_00.description: Isolating behaviors caused by unfamiliarity with the interface versus genuine neurocognitive deficits.

q_03__res_leaf_02_00_00.label: Distinguish UI confusion from cognitive deficit
q_03__res_leaf_02_00_00.description: Identify specific interface interactions (e.g., scrolling, clicking) that clinicians frequently misinterpret as memory or attention failures.

q_03__res_leaf_02_00_01.label: Impact of gamification on clinical seriousness
q_03__res_leaf_02_00_01.description: Assess if making the task look like a game reduces the clinician's confidence in the ecological validity of the results.

analogy.target_concept: Judging a stumble while walking

structural_mapping.inputs: Person walking + Observer watching closely
structural_mapping.logic: Determine if they are showing off (performing) or just walking (functioning)
structural_mapping.outputs: Valid skill assessment vs. Discarded performance noise

analogy.explanation: The researcher wants to count every stumble as a balance error (omission/commission). The expert says you must first know if they were showing off for you (Audience Effect). Just like you wouldn't call a friend clumsy if they tripped while trying to impress you, the expert won't count the error unless the patient forgets you are watching.

assistance_payload.scenario: When a patient checks your face while pouring water, do you log that hesitation as a cognitive error, or do you discard it because they were performing for you?

assistance_payload.validate_focus: The expert emphasizes that knowing a patient's historical baseline is crucial for distinguishing normal variation from symptomatic decline. This focus on premorbid context ensures that ecological tasks are interpreted against the individual's specific life history.

pivot.limitation: Focusing solely on historical baseline overlooks the potential impact of transient factors like fatigue or stress on a single observation. Without addressing temporal consistency, it remains unclear if a poor performance is due to low baseline or a temporary bad day.
pivot.research_goal: The researcher is trying to understand whether clinicians require longitudinal data to trust ecological task results. The goal is to determine if single-session validity is sufficient or if multi-session observation is necessary for clinical confidence.
pivot.compelling_reason: While historical context grounds the interpretation, establishing temporal reliability ensures that the observed behavior is stable enough to warrant clinical action. Both dimensions are needed to fully validate the screening tool in real-world settings.
pivot.coarse_scenario: I completely agree that knowing their history is vital, but I'm also trying to understand if you'd trust this task result if you only saw it once, or if you'd need to see them again to rule out a bad day.

assistance_payload.validate_focus: The expert emphasizes the difficulty of attributing behavioral interruptions to cognitive deficits versus real-world environmental noise, such as phone calls or kitchen activities. This ecological validity is crucial for understanding how NCD manifests outside the clinic.

pivot.limitation: Focusing exclusively on environmental distractions risks overlooking how digital interface complexities can themselves mimic cognitive failures during screening.
pivot.research_goal: The researcher aims to identify specific UI interactions, like scrolling or clicking errors, that clinicians might misinterpret as memory or attention deficits.
pivot.compelling_reason: Both environmental noise and interface confusion contribute to assessment validity, and isolating UI confounds ensures that digital screening tools accurately measure cognition rather than tech literacy.
pivot.coarse_scenario: I agree that knowing whether a phone ring distracts them is vital, but we also need to know if you are seeing them struggle because the screen is confusing, not because their memory is failing.

q_04__exp_L1_00.label: Expert Clinical Intuition
q_04__exp_L1_00.description: The tacit knowledge and observational heuristics experts use to detect early NCD masking during casual, pre-test interactions.

q_04__exp_L2_00_00.label: Linguistic Markers of Compensation
q_04__exp_L2_00_00.description: Specific speech patterns and narrative choices patients use to cover memory gaps during casual conversation.

q_04__exp_leaf_00_00_00.label: Empty Fluency
q_04__exp_leaf_00_00_00.description: They talk a mile a minute and sound perfectly coherent, but if you ask what they had for breakfast, they say 'oh, the usual' instead of 'eggs'—high word count, zero data.

q_04__exp_leaf_00_00_01.label: Generalizing Specific Failures
q_04__exp_leaf_00_00_01.description: You ask about a specific missed appointment and they suddenly start talking about how 'doctors are always busy' instead of admitting they forgot—shifting from specific to general to hide the slip.

q_04__exp_leaf_00_00_02.label: Emotional Deflection & Minimization
q_04__exp_leaf_00_00_02.description: They crack a joke about a 'senior moment' to normalize the error, or get defensive and irritable when pressed. Sometimes they minimize it entirely, acting like forgetting a name is 'no big deal' when it should be concerning.
aliases: act like it's no big deal

q_04__exp_leaf_00_00_03.label: Verbal Perseveration
q_04__exp_leaf_00_00_03.description: They start repeating the same story or phrase multiple times in a short conversation. It's not just forgetting they said it; it's a loop that doesn't move forward.

q_04__exp_L2_00_01.label: Interactional & Non-Verbal Dynamics
q_04__exp_L2_00_01.description: Physical and social behaviors that reveal reliance on external cues or anxiety about performance.

q_04__exp_leaf_00_01_00.label: Social Scaffolding
q_04__exp_leaf_00_01_00.description: You ask them what year it is or what they did last weekend, and their eyes dart to their wife before they answer—they're outsourcing the verification.

q_04__exp_leaf_00_01_01.label: Latency on Automatic Info
q_04__exp_leaf_00_01_01.description: Even before the test starts, if I ask their address or birthday and they hesitate, that's a red flag. That stuff should be muscle memory, so the pause tells me they're searching, not recalling.

q_04__exp_leaf_00_01_02.label: Physiological Leakage
q_04__exp_leaf_00_01_02.description: Their body language is relaxed talking about their childhood but they get stiff and fidgety when you ask about what they did this morning—the cognitive load is physically visible.

q_04__exp_leaf_00_01_03.label: Informal Command Failure
q_04__exp_leaf_00_01_03.description: During small talk, if I ask them to do two simple things like 'close the door and sit down', and they get stuck or only do one, it shows executive issues before I even pull out the MoCA.
aliases: can't follow a simple two-step instruction
nod like they understand but can't do it

q_04__exp_L2_00_02.label: Discriminating Pathology from Personality
q_04__exp_L2_00_02.description: Nuanced judgments required to distinguish masking behaviors from normal aging or lifelong personality traits.

q_04__exp_leaf_00_02_00.label: Social Polish vs Factual Accuracy
q_04__exp_leaf_00_02_00.description: They have perfect manners and chat beautifully, but can't tell me their medication list—it's the gap between social script and executive function that tips me off.
aliases: inconsistency between what they say and how they act

q_04__exp_leaf_00_02_01.label: Narrative Consistency & Flow
q_04__exp_leaf_00_02_01.description: Their stories about the past are smooth like a tape recording, but anything about today is choppy. Or they contradict themselves within the same conversation—saying one thing then another.
aliases: fragmented when talking about recent events

q_04__res_L2_00_00.description: Defining how to quantify vague intuitive cues into concrete data points.

q_04__res_L1_01.label: Linguistic & Behavioral Markers of Masking
q_04__res_L1_01.description: Specific objective cues that indicate a patient is compensating for or hiding cognitive decline during unstructured talk.

q_04__res_leaf_01_00_01.label: Reliance on social scripts to bypass cognitive load

q_04__res_leaf_01_01_01.description: Measure mismatches where a patient sounds confident but exhibits significant latency spikes, indicating compensatory processing.

q_04__res_leaf_02_00_00.description: Design dialogue systems that gently deepen topic complexity based on real-time masking detection to test limits without causing shutdown.

q_04__res_L2_02_01.label: Clinician Decision Support
q_04__res_L2_02_01.description: Visualizing intuition cues to aid diagnosis without overwhelming the user.

q_04__res_leaf_02_01_01.description: Ensure design requirements account for cultural variations in eye contact or storytelling to prevent false positives in masking detection.
